---
title: "Lesson 3: String Operations"
---

## Introduction

Strings are one of the most commonly used data types in Python. This lesson covers advanced string manipulation techniques.

## String Basics Review

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Creating strings
single = 'Hello'
double = "World"
multiline = """This is a
multiline string"""

# String concatenation
greeting = single + " " + double
print(greeting)  # Hello World
```

## String Indexing and Slicing

Strings behave like lists of characters:

```{python}
#| echo: true
text = "Python Programming"

# Indexing
print(text[0])   # P
print(text[-1])  # g

# Slicing
print(text[0:6])    # Python
print(text[7:])     # Programming
print(text[:6])     # Python
print(text[::2])    # Pto rgamn
print(text[::-1])   # gnimmargorP nohtyP
```

## String Methods

### Case Manipulation

```{python}
#| echo: true
text = "Hello World"

print(text.upper())      # HELLO WORLD
print(text.lower())      # hello world
print(text.title())      # Hello World
print(text.capitalize()) # Hello world
print(text.swapcase())   # hELLO wORLD
```

### Searching and Checking

```{python}
#| echo: true
text = "Python is awesome"

# Check if string starts/ends with
print(text.startswith("Python"))  # True
print(text.endswith("awesome"))   # True

# Find position of substring
print(text.find("is"))      # 7
print(text.find("Java"))    # -1 (not found)

# Count occurrences
print(text.count("o"))      # 2

# Check content type
print("123".isdigit())      # True
print("abc".isalpha())      # True
print("abc123".isalnum())   # True
```

### Whitespace and Alignment

```{python}
#| echo: true
text = "  Hello World  "

# Remove whitespace
print(text.strip())       # "Hello World"
print(text.lstrip())      # "Hello World  "
print(text.rstrip())      # "  Hello World"

# Alignment
print("Python".center(20, "-"))  # -------Python-------
print("Python".ljust(20, "-"))   # Python--------------
print("Python".rjust(20, "-"))   # --------------Python
```

### Splitting and Joining

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Split string into list
sentence = "Python is a great language"
words = sentence.split()
print(words)  # ['Python', 'is', 'a', 'great', 'language']

# Split with custom separator
csv_data = "apple,banana,cherry"
fruits = csv_data.split(",")
print(fruits)  # ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Join list into string
words = ["Python", "is", "awesome"]
sentence = " ".join(words)
print(sentence)  # Python is awesome

# Join with different separator
numbers = ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
result = "-".join(numbers)
print(result)  # 1-2-3-4
```

### Replacing

```{python}
#| echo: true
text = "I love Java. Java is great."

# Replace all occurrences
new_text = text.replace("Java", "Python")
print(new_text)  # I love Python. Python is great.

# Replace limited occurrences
new_text = text.replace("Java", "Python", 1)
print(new_text)  # I love Python. Java is great.
```

## String Formatting

### F-Strings (Recommended)

```{python}
#| echo: true
name = "Alice"
age = 25
height = 1.65

# Basic f-string
print(f"My name is {name}")

# Multiple variables
print(f"{name} is {age} years old")

# Expressions in f-strings
print(f"Next year {name} will be {age + 1}")

# Formatting numbers
print(f"Height: {height:.2f}m")  # 2 decimal places
print(f"Age: {age:03d}")         # Zero-padded to 3 digits
```

### Format Method

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Basic format
print("Hello, {}!".format("World"))

# Multiple placeholders
print("{} is {} years old".format("Bob", 30))

# Named placeholders
print("{name} is {age} years old".format(name="Charlie", age=35))

# Formatting
print("Pi: {:.3f}".format(3.14159))  # Pi: 3.142
```

### Old-Style Formatting

```{python}
#| echo: true
# % formatting (older style)
name = "David"
age = 28
print("My name is %s and I'm %d years old" % (name, age))
```

## Advanced String Techniques

### String Multiplication

```{python}
#| echo: true
print("=" * 50)
print("*" * 10)
print("Python! " * 3)  # Python! Python! Python! 
```

### Character Operations

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Get ASCII value
print(ord('A'))  # 65
print(ord('a'))  # 97

# Get character from ASCII
print(chr(65))   # A
print(chr(97))   # a
```

### Escape Characters

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Common escape characters
print("Line 1\nLine 2")        # Newline
print("Column1\tColumn2")      # Tab
print("He said \"Hello\"")     # Quote
print("Path: C:\\Users\\Name") # Backslash

# Raw strings (ignore escapes)
print(r"C:\Users\Name")
```

## String Immutability

Strings cannot be modified in place:

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
text = "Hello"
# text[0] = "h"  # ERROR! Strings are immutable

# Instead, create a new string
text = "h" + text[1:]
print(text)  # hello
```

## Exercises

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Exercise 1: String Analyzer

Write a program that takes a string and reports:
1. Length of the string
2. Number of uppercase letters
3. Number of lowercase letters
4. Number of digits
5. Number of spaces
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Exercise 2: Name Formatter

Create a program that:
1. Takes a full name as input (first and last name)
2. Formats it as: "LAST, First"
3. Example: "john smith" → "SMITH, John"
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3: Email Validator

Write a function that checks if an email is valid. A simple check:
1. Contains exactly one @ symbol
2. Has characters before and after @
3. The part after @ contains at least one dot
4. Has characters after the last dot

Example: "user@example.com" → Valid
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Exercise 4: Word Reverser

Create a program that:
1. Takes a sentence as input
2. Reverses each word individually (not the whole sentence)
3. Returns the modified sentence

Example: "Hello World" → "olleH dlroW"
:::

## Solutions

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution to Exercise 1

```python
def analyze_string(text):
    print(f"String: '{text}'")
    print(f"Length: {len(text)}")
    
    uppercase = sum(1 for c in text if c.isupper())
    lowercase = sum(1 for c in text if c.islower())
    digits = sum(1 for c in text if c.isdigit())
    spaces = text.count(" ")
    
    print(f"Uppercase letters: {uppercase}")
    print(f"Lowercase letters: {lowercase}")
    print(f"Digits: {digits}")
    print(f"Spaces: {spaces}")

# Test
analyze_string("Hello World 123")
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution to Exercise 2

```python
def format_name(full_name):
    # Split into parts
    parts = full_name.strip().split()
    
    if len(parts) != 2:
        return "Please enter exactly first and last name"
    
    first_name = parts[0]
    last_name = parts[1]
    
    # Format: LAST, First
    formatted = f"{last_name.upper()}, {first_name.capitalize()}"
    return formatted

# Test
print(format_name("john smith"))    # SMITH, John
print(format_name("ALICE JONES"))   # JONES, Alice
print(format_name("bob WILLIAMS"))  # WILLIAMS, Bob
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution to Exercise 3

```python
def is_valid_email(email):
    # Check for exactly one @
    if email.count("@") != 1:
        return False
    
    # Split at @
    parts = email.split("@")
    local = parts[0]
    domain = parts[1]
    
    # Check local part has content
    if len(local) == 0:
        return False
    
    # Check domain has at least one dot
    if "." not in domain:
        return False
    
    # Check there's content after the last dot
    domain_parts = domain.split(".")
    if len(domain_parts[-1]) == 0:
        return False
    
    return True

# Test
print(is_valid_email("user@example.com"))     # True
print(is_valid_email("invalid.email.com"))    # False
print(is_valid_email("@example.com"))         # False
print(is_valid_email("user@domain"))          # False
print(is_valid_email("user@domain."))         # False
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution to Exercise 4

```python
def reverse_words(sentence):
    # Split into words
    words = sentence.split()
    
    # Reverse each word
    reversed_words = [word[::-1] for word in words]
    
    # Join back together
    result = " ".join(reversed_words)
    return result

# Test
print(reverse_words("Hello World"))           # olleH dlroW
print(reverse_words("Python Programming"))    # nohtyP gnimmargorP
print(reverse_words("The quick brown fox"))   # ehT kciuq nworb xof
```
:::

## Summary

In this lesson, you learned:

- ✅ String indexing and slicing
- ✅ Essential string methods
- ✅ String formatting with f-strings and format()
- ✅ String immutability and workarounds
- ✅ Advanced string manipulation techniques

## Module 2 Complete! 🎉

Congratulations! You've completed Module 2 and learned about Python's built-in data structures.

Continue to [Module 3: Control Flow and Functions](../module3/_index.qmd) to learn about conditional statements, loops, and functions!
